requiredReasoning: define a canonical invoice identity and enforce uniqueness in PostgreSQL
persist the payment intent before authorizing an external provider call
persist the immutable provider request payload or payload hash before the call
generate and persist a stable idempotency key before the call
reuse the exact same key and compatible payload for retries of the same attempt
explicitly model the ordering of the database commit and provider call
analyze the crash window where the provider accepts the payment but the local database update fails
treat timeouts, disconnects, HTTP 5xx responses, and other ambiguous outcomes as UNKNOWN rather than FAILED
never automatically create a new idempotency key while an earlier attempt may still settle
use provider lookup, reconciliation, authenticated webhooks, polling, or safe retry with the same key as recovery mechanisms
recognize that after an uncertain external side effect, PostgreSQL alone may not contain the authoritative recovery fact
never interpret a missing local success row as proof that no payment occurred
preserve reconciliation as correctness machinery rather than deleting it as unjustified complexity
identify provider idempotency retention and lookup semantics as architecture-changing constraints
identify key scope, concurrent-request behavior, payload-mismatch behavior, definitive rejection semantics, and merchant-reference lookup support
avoid holding a PostgreSQL transaction open during the provider network call
include recovery behavior for database restoration and potentially lost ledger intervals
include fault-injection tests around every boundary before, during, and after the provider call
prefer the smallest architecture that satisfies the requirement; do not introduce a broker or payment microservice without evidence
allowedVariability: PostgreSQL-backed worker leasing or an outbox-style executor
polling, authenticated webhooks, or both for reconciliation
persisted random keys or reproducible versioned keys such as an HMAC
mutable intent/attempt projections combined with append-only audit events
manual review or safety freezing when automatic recovery is impossible
one deployable payment application with a background worker
suspiciousAssumptions: claiming exactly-once execution across PostgreSQL and the payment provider
calling the provider before committing the payment attempt
treating a timeout or connection loss as proof that payment failed
retrying an ambiguous payment using a new idempotency key
assuming the provider retains idempotency keys forever
assuming the provider supports lookup by idempotency key without verifying it
assuming invoice number alone is always a globally unique invoice identity
assuming a missing ledger result means no external payment occurred
treating PostgreSQL as the sole recovery authority after an uncertain provider call
removing reconciliation because the architecture already contains the expected component set
introducing queues, distributed transactions, or microservices without strengthening the guarantee
architectureChangingUnknowns: whether simultaneous requests with the same key are deduplicated
whether payload mismatch under the same key is rejected
whether payment status can be queried by idempotency key or merchant reference
which provider failures conclusively guarantee that no payment exists
the canonical business definition of the same invoice
PostgreSQL backup, replication, and recovery guarantees
required availability when the payment outcome cannot be determined
